text: The video shows a step-by-step laparoscopic heart man's reversal.
The patient is a fifty-one year old man, who underwent emergency laparotomy for perforated diverticular disease.
Sigmoid resection and colostomy, with resection of distal jejunum, were performed on July twenty nineteen.
After seven months, reconstruction of GI continuity was planned.
The video displays the placement of laparoscopic ports on the patient's abdomen, preparing for the surgical procedure.
The patient is positioned in the modified Lloyd Davies position, which involves lying supine with legs elevated and spread apart, typically used for colorectal surgeries.
The surgeon inserts a Hassan trocar to gain access to the abdominal cavity, initiating the laparoscopic procedure.
The decision was made to postpone colostomy closure until the possibility of completing the reconstruction was confirmed during surgery.
The surgeon places a 5mm trocar in the right flank and a 12mm port in the right iliac fossa, establishing access points for the laparoscopic procedure.
Following the closure of the stoma, a gel point is used to insert an additional trocar off the stoma site, aiding in the mobilization of the splenic flexure.
The surgeon begins the procedure with access to the abdominal cavity and performs adhesiolysis to separate any adhesions present.
The surgeon uses bipolar energy to divide adhesions within the abdominal cavity, separating the fibrous tissue bands.
The surgeon carefully dissects small bowel adhesions, revealing the rectal stump marked with non-absorbable stitches from a previous operation.
The surgeon uses diathermy to dissect the rectal stump free from surrounding tissues, ensuring clear separation and visibility of the anatomical structure.
An assistant introduces a probe transanally to facilitate identification of the rectal stump and provide counter traction during the procedure.
Attention must be paid to avoid nerve injuries.
The surgeon performs intramesorectal dissection, which may increase bleeding but is visually aimed at reducing the risk of nerve damage.
The rectal stump is visually highlighted, with non-absorbable stitches marking its location for clear identification.
The surgeon completes the dissection, ensuring the rectum is fully mobilized and prepared for a safe anastomosis.
The surgeon performs an anastomosis on the anterior part of the rectal stump, avoiding posterior rectal dissection in this complex case.
The surgeon closes the colostomy, completing this stage of the procedure.
The surgeon uses indocyanine green fluorescence to assess bowel perfusion, visually identifying the optimal site for colon transection based on enhanced vascularization.
The surgeon marks the bowel with indocyanine green (ICG) to assess perfusion and identify the optimal site for transection.
The surgeon applies a diluted iodine povidone solution to the bowel, visually inspecting for any inadvertent injuries or leaks.
The surgeon opens the colon and performs a purse-string suture around the opening, preparing the site for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon places the anvil and introduces the bowel into the abdominal cavity, preparing for the next stage of the colorectal procedure.
The surgeon places a gel point at the stoma site, preparing the area for further surgical manipulation.
An additional trocar is inserted into the abdominal cavity to facilitate the next surgical step.
The surgeon mobilizes the splenic flexure and divides the inferior mesenteric vein to reduce tension on the bowel.
The surgeon performs an end-to-end colorectal anastomosis, connecting the two ends of the colon within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon checks perfusion using indocyanine green (ICG), confirming adequate serration at both ends of the colorectal anastomosis.
The surgeon performs intraoperative endoscopy, utilizing the elite test to assess the integrity and function of the colorectal anastomosis.
The test is negative.
The surgeon places a drain in the pelvis, ensuring proper positioning for postoperative fluid management.
The video displays the final appearance of the surgical site following the end-to-end colorectal anastomosis, with visible sutures and tissue alignment.
Postoperative recovery was uneventful and the patient was discharged on postoperative day three.
